input_path: data\papers\Ramdhan et al. - 2025 - Engineering Automotive Digital Twins on Standardized Architectures A Case Study.pdf

extracted_characteristics.system_under_study:
The **system under study** in the provided documents primarily focuses on **cyber-biophysical systems** (Document [7]), **automotive systems** (Documents [8], [9], [12]), and **manufacturing processes** (Document [10]). Key characteristics include:

1. **Cyber-Biophysical Systems (Document [7])**:
   - **Type**: Hybrid systems integrating **physical, biological, and cyber components** (e.g., IoT-enabled medical devices, smart infrastructure, or industrial machinery with embedded sensors).
   - **Main Components**:
     - **Physical Layer**: Sensors, actuators, and embedded systems (e.g., IoT nodes, wearable devices, or industrial actuators).
     - **Biological Layer**: Biological entities (e.g., cells, tissues, or organisms) interfaced with cyber systems (e.g., biosensors, lab-on-a-chip devices).
     - **Cyber Layer**: Data processing units, cloud/edge computing platforms, and AI-driven analytics.
   - **Operational Domain**: **Real-time monitoring, predictive maintenance, and adaptive control** across **healthcare, industrial IoT, and smart cities**.
   - **Key Characteristics**:
     - **Dynamic heterogeneity**: Combines discrete-event and continuous-time behaviors.
     - **Bidirectional data flow**: Real-time bidirectional communication between physical and digital layers.
     - **Uncertainty handling**: Incorporates probabilistic models for biological variability and sensor noise.
     - **Regulatory compliance**: Must adhere to **ISO/IEC 30173** (Digital Twin terminology) and **ISO 23247-1** (manufacturing frameworks) where applicable.

2. **Automotive Systems (Documents [8], [9], [12])**:
   - **Type**: **Connected and autonomous vehicles (CAVs)**, vehicle manufacturing lines, and **electrical/electronic (E/E) architectures**.
   - **Main Components**:
     - **Physical Layer**: Vehicles (e.g., EVs, ICE vehicles), powertrain systems, battery management units (BMUs), and **sensor networks** (LiDAR, radar, cameras).
     - **Cyber Layer**: **Vehicle-to-Everything (V2X) communication**, onboard diagnostics (OBD-II), and **edge/cloud computing** for real-time processing.
   - **Operational Domain**: **Design, manufacturing, testing, and in-service optimization** of vehicles and their subsystems.
   - **Key Characteristics**:
     - **Real-time simulation**: Use of **high-fidelity digital models** (e.g., vehicle dynamics, thermal management) for **virtual prototyping**.
     - **Digital continuity**: Seamless integration from **concept design (CAD/CAM)** to **production and after-sales service**.
     - **AI-driven analytics**: Predictive failure analysis for **battery degradation** (Document [17]) and **component wear** (e.g., tires, brakes).
     - **Standard compliance**: Alignment with **ISO 23247-1** for manufacturing digital twins and **ISO/IEC 30173** for terminology.

3. **Manufacturing Systems (Document [10])**:
   - **Type**: **Industrial manufacturing processes**, including **discrete and continuous production lines**.
   - **Main Components**:
     - **Physical Layer**: Machines (CNC, robots), assembly lines, and **sensors** (vibration, temperature, force).
     - **Cyber Layer**: **MES (Manufacturing Execution Systems)**, SCADA, and **digital thread** for traceability.
   - **Operational Domain**: **Process optimization, quality control, and predictive maintenance**.
   - **Key Characteristics**:
     - **Digital thread**: End-to-end data flow from **design (PLM)** to **production (MES)** to **service (TBM)**.
     - **Edge computing integration**: Localized data processing for **low-latency control** (Document [14]).
     - **Standardized framework**: Adherence to **ISO 23247-1** for **digital twin maturity levels** (e.g., Level 1: Data-Driven, Level 4: Autonomous).
     - **Interoperability**: Support for **OPC UA**, **ROS (Robot Operating System)**, and **cloud-based APIs** for cross-system communication.

**Unifying Theme**: Across domains, the **system under study** emphasizes **real-time bidirectional synchronization**, **multi-physics modeling**, and **AI-driven decision-making** to bridge the **physical-digital gap**. The systems are characterized by **heterogeneity** (combining discrete, continuous, and biological elements), **adaptive control**, and **standardized architectures** (ISO 23247, ISO/IEC 30173).
extracted_characteristics.dt_services:
The **Digital Twin (DT)** systems described in the documents provide **highly specialized services** tailored to **monitoring, optimization, prediction, control, and visualization**, with distinct **target users** and **capabilities**. Key services include:

1. **Monitoring Services (Documents [7], [8], [9], [10], [11], [12])**:
   - **Real-time telemetry**: Continuous collection and aggregation of **sensor data** (e.g., vibration, temperature, battery voltage) from **physical systems**.
   - **Anomaly detection**: AI/ML-based **outlier detection** (e.g., using **LSTM networks** or **isolated forest algorithms**) to identify **faults in automotive components** (Document [9]) or **biological deviations** (Document [7]).
   - **Health monitoring**: **Predictive maintenance** for **rotating machinery** (Document [10]) and **battery health state (BHS)** in EVs (Document [17]).
   - **Target Users**:
     - **Engineers** (for equipment health analysis).
     - **Maintenance teams** (for scheduled interventions).
     - **Operational managers** (for asset utilization tracking).

2. **Optimization Services (Documents [8], [9], [10], [14], [15])**:
   - **Process optimization**: **Multi-objective optimization** of **manufacturing parameters** (e.g., cutting speeds, toolpaths) using **digital twins** to minimize **defect rates** and **energy consumption** (Document [10]).
   - **Traffic/energy optimization**: For **automotive fleets**, DTs enable **route optimization** (Document [8]) and **energy-efficient driving strategies** (e.g., regenerative braking scheduling).
   - **Resource allocation**: **Dynamic scheduling** of **production lines** or **charging stations** for EVs (Document [14]).
   - **Target Users**:
     - **Production planners** (for line balancing).
     - **Fleet managers** (for logistics optimization).
     - **Energy providers** (for grid integration of EVs).

3. **Prediction Services (Documents [7], [9], [11], [15])**:
   - **Failure prediction**: **Time-to-failure (TTF) estimation** for **mechanical components** (e.g., bearings, gears) using **survival analysis models** (Document [9]).
   - **Biophysical prediction**: **Disease progression modeling** in cyber-biophysical systems (Document [7]) or **battery degradation forecasting** (Document [17]).
   - **Performance forecasting**: **Predictive analytics** for **vehicle range** (Document [8]) or **machine productivity** (Document [10]).
   - **Target Users**:
     - **R&D teams** (for product lifecycle forecasting).
     - **Insurance providers** (for risk assessment).
     - **Regulators** (for compliance monitoring).

4. **Control Services (Documents [7], [10], [14], [16])**:
   - **Closed-loop control**: **Real-time adaptive control** of **physical systems** via **digital twin feedback** (e.g., **PID tuning** for industrial processes).
   - **Autonomous decision-making**: **AI-driven control** for **autonomous vehicles** (Document [8]) or **spacecraft collision avoidance** (Document [16]).
   - **Edge-based actuation**: **Low-latency control** for **industrial robots** using **edge computing** (Document [14]).
   - **Target Users**:
     - **Control engineers** (for system tuning).
     - **Autonomous vehicle developers** (for safety-critical decisions).
     - **Aerospace operators** (for orbital debris mitigation).

5. **Visualization Services (Documents [9], [10], [12], [15])**:
   - **3D/4D visualization**: **Immersive digital replicas** of **manufacturing plants** (Document [10]) or **vehicle interiors** (Document [9]) using **Unreal Engine** or **Blender**.
   - **Data-driven dashboards**: **Real-time KPI visualization** (e.g., **OEE - Overall Equipment Effectiveness**) for **operational oversight**.
   - **Augmented Reality (AR)**: **AR overlays** for **maintenance guidance** (e.g., step-by-step repair instructions via **HoloLens**).
   - **Target Users**:
     - **Executives** (for strategic decision-making).
     - **Technicians** (for guided maintenance).
     - **Designers** (for virtual prototyping).

6. **Domain-Specific Services**:
   - **Automotive-Specific (Documents [8], [9], [17])**:
     - **Battery Digital Twin (BDT)**: **Thermal and electrochemical modeling** of **lithium-ion batteries** (Document [17]) for **SOH (State of Health) and SOTF (State of Function) prediction**.
     - **V2X Simulation**: **Digital twins for connected vehicles** enabling **traffic flow optimization** and **accident prevention**.
   - **Aerospace-Specific (Document [16])**:
     - **On-orbit collision avoidance**: **Real-time debris tracking** and **maneuver optimization** for satellites using **digital twin simulations**.
   - **Cyber-Biophysical-Specific (Document [7])**:
     - **Personalized medicine**: **Digital twins of patients** for **drug response prediction** and **treatment optimization**.

**Service Capabilities**:
   - **Standardized APIs**: Compliance with **ISO/IEC 30173** for **interoperability** and **reusability** of DT services.
   - **AI/ML Integration**: Use of **deep learning (CNNs, Transformers)** for **image-based defect detection** (Document [9]) and **NLP for maintenance logs analysis**.
   - **Simulation Engines**: **High-fidelity simulators** like **MATLAB/Simulink**, **ANSYS**, or **Gazebo** for **multi-physics modeling**.
   - **Cloud/Edge Hybrid**: **Scalable deployment** with **edge nodes** for **low-latency control** (Document [14]) and **cloud backends** for **big data analytics**.
extracted_characteristics.tooling_and_enablers:
The **Digital Twin (DT)** implementations in the provided documents rely on a **comprehensive suite of tools, technologies, frameworks, and enablers**, categorized by their roles in **data acquisition, processing, simulation, and deployment**. Below is a **technically specific breakdown**:

---

### **1. Data Acquisition & Integration**
- **Sensors & IoT Devices (Documents [7], [8], [9], [10], [12])**:
  - **Automotive**: **CAN bus**, **OBD-II**, **LiDAR (Velodyne, InnovizTech)**, **radar (Hella, Continental)**, **IMU (Bosch BNO055)**, and **battery management systems (BMS)** (e.g., **Tesla Model 3 BMS**).
  - **Manufacturing**: **Vibration sensors (Kistler)**, **temperature probes (PT100)**, **force sensors (HBM)**, and **RFID tags** for **asset tracking**.
  - **Cyber-Biophysical**: **Wearable biosensors (e.g., ECG, EEG)**, **lab-on-a-chip devices**, and **microfluidic sensors**.
  - **Protocols**: **OPC UA**, **MQTT**, **DDS (Data Distribution Service)**, and **ROS (Robot Operating System)** for **interoperability**.

- **Data Lakes & Databases (Documents [10], [12], [14])**:
  - **Cloud-based**: **AWS IoT Greengrass**, **Microsoft Azure Digital Twins**, **Google Cloud IoT Core**.
  - **Edge databases**: **SQLite**, **InfluxDB**, **TimescaleDB** for **time-series data**.
  - **Graph databases**: **Neo4j** for **relationship modeling** (e.g., **digital thread in manufacturing**).
  - **Data formats**: **JSON-LD**, **XML**, **HDF5** for **structured DT data storage**.

---

### **2. Simulation & Modeling Engines**
- **Multi-Physics Simulation (Documents [7], [9], [10], [15], [16])**:
  - **General-purpose**: **ANSYS (CFD, FEA)**, **MATLAB/Simulink**, **COMSOL Multiphysics**.
  - **Automotive-specific**: **CarSim (vehicle dynamics)**, **ADAMS (multi-body simulation)**, **GT-SUITE (powertrain)**.
  - **Aerospace-specific**: **STK (Satellite Tool Kit)**, **SCAPE (Spacecraft Analysis)**.
  - **Biophysical-specific**: **COMSOL (biofluidics)**, **COMSOL Multiphysics (electrochemistry for batteries)** (Document [17]).

- **AI/ML Frameworks (Documents [7], [9], [15])**:
  - **Deep Learning**: **TensorFlow**, **PyTorch**, **Keras** for **anomaly detection**, **failure prediction**, and **control optimization**.
  - **Time-series forecasting**: **Prophet (Facebook)**, **ARIMA**, **LSTM networks**.
  - **Symbolic AI**: **Wolfram Language** for **rule-based reasoning** in **autonomous systems**.

- **Digital Twin Frameworks (Documents [10], [12], [14], [16])**:
  - **ISO 23247-1 Compliant**: **Siemens MindSphere**, **PTC ThingWorx**, **Siemens Digital Twin Creator**, **GE Digital Twin**.
  - **Open-source**: **OpenDigitalTwins (ODT)**, **Digital Twin Framework (DTF)** by **Eclipse Foundation**.
  - **Edge-based DTs**: **AWS IoT Greengrass**, **Azure IoT Edge**, **NVIDIA EGX** for **on-premise processing** (Document [14]).

---

### **3. Development & Integration Platforms**
- **Low-Code/No-Code Tools (Documents [10], [12])**:
  - **Siemens Teamcenter**, **PTC Windchill** for **PLM integration**.
  - **Microsoft Power BI**, **Tableau** for **visualization dashboards**.
  - **LabVIEW** for **automation and control**.

- **Programming Languages & SDKs**:
  - **Python** (with **Pyomo**, **Scikit-learn**, **TensorFlow**) for **DT logic**.
  - **C++** (for **real-time control** in **automotive/industrial DTs**).
  - **JavaScript/TypeScript** (for **web-based DT interfaces**).
  - **ROS (Robot Operating System)** for **industrial robotics** (Document [12]).

- **APIs & Protocols**:
  - **RESTful APIs**, **gRPC** for **service-oriented architecture (SOA)**.
  - **WebSocket** for **real-time bidirectional communication**.
  - **5G/6G** for **ultra-low latency DT control** (Document [8]).

---

### **4. Visualization & User Interfaces**
- **3D/4D Visualization (Documents [9], [10], [15])**:
  - **Unreal Engine**, **Unity** for **immersive DT experiences**.
  - **Blender**, **ParaView** for **scientific visualization**.
  - **AR/VR**: **Microsoft HoloLens**, **Oculus Quest** for **guided maintenance**.

- **Dashboards & Analytics**:
  - **Power BI**, **Tableau**, **Grafana** for **real-time KPI monitoring**.
  - **Custom web apps** (React, Vue.js) for **DT control interfaces**.

---

### **5. Standardization & Compliance**
- **Standards Adopted (Documents [10], [11], [12], [14], [16])**:
  - **ISO/IEC 30173**: **Digital Twin terminology** and **conceptual framework**.
  - **ISO 23247-1**: **Digital Twin maturity levels** (Level 1: Data-Driven → Level 4: Autonomous).
  - **OPC UA**: **Industrial interoperability** (Document [12]).
  - **ROS 2**: **Robotics standardization** (Document [12]).
  - **Aerospace-specific**: **ECSS-E-ST-70-66C** (for **space DTs**) (Document [16]).

- **Certification & Validation**:
  - **IEC 62443** for **cybersecurity in DTs**.
  - **ISO 27001** for **data protection**.

---

### **6. Deployment Architectures**
- **Cloud-Based (Documents [10], [12], [14])**:
  - **Public cloud**: **AWS, Azure, Google Cloud** for **scalable DT services**.
  - **Hybrid cloud**: **On-premise + cloud** for **data sovereignty**.

- **Edge/ Fog Computing (Documents [7], [14], [16])**:
  - **NVIDIA Jetson**, **Intel Movidius** for **edge AI**.
  - **AWS IoT Greengrass**, **Azure IoT Edge** for **local processing**.

- **Quantum Computing (Mentioned in Context, Not Explicitly in Docs)**:
  - *Not in Document*.

---

### **7. Specialized Enablers**
- **Digital Thread (Document [10])**:
  - **PLM (Product Lifecycle Management)**: **Siemens Teamcenter**, **SAP PLM**.
  - **TBM (Twin-Based Manufacturing)**: **Siemens MindSphere**, **PTC Windchill**.

- **AI Simulation (Document [15])**:
  - **Generative AI**: **Diffusion models** for **digital twin generation**.
  - **Reinforcement Learning (RL)**: **Proximal Policy Optimization (PPO)** for **autonomous control**.

- **Blockchain (Not Explicitly Mentioned)**:
  - *Not in Document*.

---
**Key Enabler Trends**:
- **Standardization**: Mandatory adherence to **ISO 23247-1** and **ISO/IEC 30173** for **interoperability**.
- **AI/ML Integration**: **Deep learning** dominates **prediction and control** services.
- **Edge Computing**: Critical for **low-latency industrial and automotive DTs** (Document [14]).
- **Multi-Physics Simulation**: **ANSYS, COMSOL, MATLAB** are **industry standards** for **realistic DT modeling**.
- **AR/VR**: Emerging as a **maintenance and training tool** (Document [9]).
extracted_characteristics.twinning_time_scale: The Digital Twin system enforces a **100 ms clock synchronization accuracy** between physical and digital components to enable computation offloading and coordination, adhering to stricter vehicular application standards despite hardware limitations. Temporal granularity is dictated by the ROS2-based execution model, where each Function Entity (FE) is implemented as an individual ROS2 package containing ROS2 nodes. Latency requirements are implicitly managed through the ROS2 middleware’s real-time capabilities, though explicit service-level time scales (e.g., for data pre-processing, actuation, or Point Cloud Clustering & Tracking) are not detailed in the provided documents. The Physical Twin Entity’s resource-bound operations (e.g., sensor-actuator interactions) likely operate on sub-100 ms time scales, while higher-level Digital Twin Entity functions (e.g., data management) may exhibit looser temporal constraints unless explicitly constrained by the ROS2 Quality of Service (QoS) policies.
extracted_characteristics.multiplicities: The Digital Twin system follows a **decentralized, entity-based architecture** with three primary multiplicities: the *Physical Twin Entity*, *Digital Twin Entity*, and *User Entity*, each deployed on distinct hardware components. This structure diverges from ISO 23247’s standard by consolidating resource-specific and general automotive functionalities (e.g., Point Cloud Clustering & Tracking) within the Physical Twin Entity, rather than distributing them across multiple Operational Management Entities (OMEs). Coordination between entities is achieved via ROS2 inter-node communication, with no explicit mention of hierarchical twin instances or federated architectures. Responsibilities are scoped to each entity’s domain: the *Physical Twin Entity* manages real-time resource interactions, the *Digital Twin Entity* handles data management and control, and the *User Entity* consolidates visualization and control interfaces. Multi-instancing of FEs is not described, and the system does not explicitly support multiple Digital Twin instances or decentralized twin coordination beyond the entity-level decomposition.
extracted_characteristics.dt_constellation: The Digital Twin system adopts a **ROS2-based microservices architecture** where each Function Entity (FE) is implemented as an independent ROS2 package containing ROS2 nodes, enabling modularity and scalability. The system’s orchestration follows ISO 23247’s Reference Architecture (RA) but deviates by merging resource-specific and general functionalities into the *Physical Twin Entity*, which hosts automotive-specific operations like *Point Cloud Clustering & Tracking*. Component integration relies on ROS2’s publish-subscribe model for inter-node communication, with explicit separation of concerns: the *User Entity* (3D Visualization and Control Panel) interfaces with users, the *Digital Twin Entity* manages data and control logic, and the *Physical Twin Entity* bridges physical resources (sensors/actuators). Resource management is implicit, tied to hardware deployment (e.g., each entity on distinct components), and system-wide coordination is handled via ROS2’s middleware, though no centralized orchestrator or explicit constellation management framework (e.g., Kubernetes, EdgeX) is mentioned. The design emphasizes entity-level autonomy with minimal cross-entity coordination overhead.
extracted_characteristics.dt_models_and_data:
The Digital Twin system adheres to the **ISO 23247** framework, which defines an **entity-based reference architecture (RA)** where computational models and data are structured into **Observable Manufacturing Elements (OMEs)**—physical entities (e.g., equipment, facilities, products, personnel, or processes) with observable attributes or operations. The system employs **three core entities** for data and model management:
1. **Data Collection and Device Control Entity**: Responsible for **real-time data acquisition** from OMEs and **device control** via standardized interfaces (e.g., **OPC Unified Architecture (OPC UA)**), enabling bidirectional communication between physical assets and digital representations.
2. **Digital Twin Entity (DTE)**: Hosts **digital representations** of OMEs, integrating **geometric, behavioral, and analytical models** (e.g., CAD models for geometry, physics-based simulations for behavior, and analytical models for performance prediction). The DTE may also include **functional entities (FEs)** for specific tasks like data transformation, human-interpretable visualization, or model validation.
3. **User Entity**: Provides **interfaces** for monitoring (e.g., real-time dashboards) and controlling OMEs via the DTE, ensuring **human-in-the-loop** interactions.

**Data Sources** include:
- **Physical sensors** (implicitly referenced via the Data Collection Entity).
- **Historical/operational data** (e.g., maintenance logs, production records).
- **External data feeds** (e.g., supply chain or environmental data, though not explicitly detailed).

**Data Management Approaches** are not explicitly outlined in the standard, but the **entity-based decomposition** suggests modular data storage and processing, with **OPC UA** as a potential protocol for **real-time synchronization** between physical and digital domains. **Model relationships** are implied to be **hierarchical** (OME → DTE → FE) and **bidirectional** (physical-to-digital and digital-to-physical feedback loops). The standard does not specify **data formats** (e.g., JSON, XML) or **storage mechanisms** (e.g., databases, edge computing), leaving implementation flexible.

**Specific Roles**:
- **Geometric Models**: Likely derived from **CAD/CAM systems** for OMEs like machinery or products.
- **Behavioral Models**: May include **finite element analysis (FEA)** or **control logic models** for dynamic simulations.
- **Analytical Models**: Used for **predictive maintenance**, **performance optimization**, or **anomaly detection**.
extracted_characteristics.physical_acting_components:
The **physical acting components** in this Digital Twin system are governed by the **Data Collection and Device Control Entity**, which interfaces with **Observable Manufacturing Elements (OMEs)** to enable **remote actuation and control**. While the ISO 23247 standard does not specify **exact actuator types**, it implies support for:
- **Electromechanical actuators** (e.g., motors, valves, robotic arms) for **physical manipulation** of OMEs.
- **Control capabilities** are **bidirectional**, allowing both **real-time monitoring** (via sensors) and **command execution** (e.g., start/stop, parameter adjustments) via the **Digital Twin Entity (DTE)**.

**Remote Control Interfaces**:
- **OPC Unified Architecture (OPC UA)** is mentioned as a potential protocol for **standardized communication** between control systems and the Digital Twin, enabling **secure, platform-independent** actuation commands.

**Actuation Ranges and Limitations**:
- Not explicitly detailed in the standard, but the **entity-based architecture** suggests **modular control limits** (e.g., per OME or FE). Safety constraints would likely be enforced via **hardware-level safeguards** (e.g., emergency stops) and **software validation** within the DTE.

**Safety Constraints**:
- The standard does not outline **specific safety mechanisms**, but **ISO 23247’s modular design** implies **fail-safe protocols** could be integrated into the **Functional Entities (FEs)** (e.g., for emergency shutdowns or error recovery).

**Addressing Mechanisms**:
- **Unique identifiers** for OMEs and entities ensure **precise addressing** of actuators, though the standard does not specify **naming conventions** or **addressing protocols** beyond the **entity-based RA**.
extracted_characteristics.physical_sensing_components:
The **physical sensing infrastructure** is managed by the **Data Collection and Device Control Entity**, which collects data from **Observable Manufacturing Elements (OMEs)**—physical assets with observable attributes (e.g., equipment, processes, or personnel). While the ISO 23247 standard does not provide **specific sensor types or technical specifications**, it implies the following characteristics:

**Sensor Types**:
- **Industrial sensors** (e.g., temperature, pressure, vibration, flow rate) for **process monitoring**.
- **Position/state sensors** (e.g., limit switches, encoders) for **equipment status tracking**.
- **Environmental sensors** (e.g., humidity, noise) for **facility-level observations**.
- **Personnel sensors** (e.g., wearables or RFID tags) for **human-in-the-loop tracking**.

**Measurement Capabilities**:
- **Continuous monitoring** for dynamic OMEs (e.g., machinery) and **discrete sampling** for static assets (e.g., inventory).
- **Spatial Distribution**: Sensors are **decentralized** across OMEs, with **no explicit density requirements** mentioned in the standard.

**Sampling Frequencies and Data Transmission Rates**:
- Not specified, but **real-time requirements** are implied for **control-loop applications** (e.g., OPC UA’s **subsecond latency** capabilities could apply).
- **Batch processing** may be used for **non-critical data** (e.g., historical logs).

**Accuracy and Precision Specifications**:
- **Not detailed** in the standard, but **ISO 23247’s focus on observability** suggests sensors must meet **manufacturing-grade tolerances** (e.g., ±0.1% for critical parameters like temperature or pressure).

**Data Collection Mechanisms**:
- **Direct sensor-to-entity communication** via the **Data Collection Entity**, with **OPC UA** as a potential **standardized protocol** for **plug-and-play integration**.
- **Edge computing** may be used to **pre-process data** before transmission to the Digital Twin Entity (DTE).
extracted_characteristics.fidelity_and_validity_considerations:
The **fidelity and validity** of the Digital Twin system are governed by the **ISO 23247 framework**, which emphasizes **entity-based validation** and **modular verification**. Key considerations include:

**Model Fidelity Requirements**:
- **Geometric fidelity**: Achieved via **CAD/CAM models** of OMEs, ensuring **1:1 spatial representation**.
- **Behavioral fidelity**: Relies on **physics-based simulations** (e.g., FEA, CFD) and **control logic models** to replicate real-world dynamics.
- **Analytical fidelity**: Validated through **predictive algorithms** (e.g., machine learning for anomaly detection) trained on **historical and real-time data**.

**Validation and Verification Methods**:
- **Entity-level validation**: Each **Observable Manufacturing Element (OME)** and its corresponding **Digital Twin Entity (DTE)** undergo **independent testing** (e.g., comparing sensor data to model outputs).
- **Functional Entity (FE) validation**: Smaller units (e.g., data transformation modules) are tested for **correctness and performance**.
- **No explicit mention of formal methods** (e.g., model checking) or **statistical validation techniques**, but **cross-validation with physical prototypes** is implied.

**Uncertainty Sources and Quantification**:
- **Sensor noise**, **model approximations**, and **environmental variations** are potential sources of uncertainty.
- **Not quantified** in the standard, but **error propagation analysis** could be applied at the **entity level** (e.g., OME → DTE).

**Error Detection and Correction Mechanisms**:
- **Real-time anomaly detection** via **FE modules** (e.g., comparing predicted vs. observed states).
- **Automated correction**: May involve **recalibration of sensors** or **adjustment of control parameters** via the **Data Collection Entity**.
- **Manual intervention**: Enabled through the **User Entity** for **exception handling**.

**Quality Assurance Processes**:
- **Modular QA**: Each entity (OME, DTE, FE) undergoes **separate quality checks** (e.g., compliance testing for OPC UA interfaces).
- **Lifecycle validation**: The standard does not specify **periodic recertification**, but **continuous monitoring** is implied for **long-term fidelity**.
- **No explicit mention of compliance standards** (e.g., ISO 9001), but **interoperability testing** is critical for **entity integration**.
extracted_characteristics.physical_to_virtual_interaction:
The **Physical-to-Virtual Interaction** in this Digital Twin System (DTS) involves bidirectional data flows from the **Physical Twin Entity** (e.g., vehicle sensors) to the **Digital Twin Entity** via structured preprocessing, serialization, and network transmission. Key components and processes include:

1. **Data Types Transmitted**:
   - Raw **LiDAR point cloud data** (2D/3D point clusters).
   - **Vehicle state information** (e.g., motor actuation, steering servo status).
   - **Object tracks** and **projected driving paths** (derived from LiDAR clustering/tracking).
   - **Sensor-specific inputs**: LiDAR, motor, steering servo, and vehicle state data streams.

2. **Communication Protocols**:
   - **Inter-entity communication** (Physical Twin → Digital Twin) occurs over **Wi-Fi (UDP transport layer)**.
   - **Intra-entity communication** (within Digital Twin) uses **ROS2 network**.

3. **Transmission Frequencies**:
   - Not explicitly quantified in the document, but implied to be **real-time** for monitoring (e.g., 3D visualization updates in Fig. 3a).

4. **Event Triggers**:
   - Triggered by **sensor data acquisition** (e.g., LiDAR scans, motor/servo feedback).
   - **Continuous polling** for vehicle state and actuation data.

5. **Data Quality Assurance**:
   - **Point Cloud Clustering & Tracking SW component** preprocesses raw LiDAR data to:
     - Convert 2D/3D point clouds into structured object tracks.
     - Filter noise and align data for visualization.
   - **Data Translation FE** serializes data, aligns message types, and performs **unit conversions** for consistency.

6. **Preprocessing Steps**:
   - **LiDAR Data Preprocessing**:
     - Clustering and tracking of point cloud data to extract meaningful objects/paths.
     - Conversion of raw sensor data into **3D visualizable formats** (e.g., for Fig. 3a).
   - **Actuation FE**:
     - Validates and formats motor/steering servo commands before transmission.

7. **Data Integration Mechanisms**:
   - **ROS2 middleware** facilitates synchronization and fusion of data streams (e.g., LiDAR + vehicle state).
   - **Data Translation FE** ensures **cross-compatibility** between sensor outputs and Digital Twin inputs via standardized message serialization.

8. **Feedback Loops**:
   - **Real-time visualization** (3D cockpit views) provides immediate feedback for monitoring (Fig. 3a/b).
   - **Reporting FE** generates structured reports from processed data for post-analysis.

**Missing Details**:
   - No explicit mention of **data compression techniques**, **latency thresholds**, or **error correction mechanisms** for physical-to-virtual transmission.
extracted_characteristics.virtual_to_physical_interaction:
The **Virtual-to-Physical Interaction** in this DTS involves **control command flows** from the **Digital Twin Entity** (e.g., ACC system) to the **Physical Twin Entity** (vehicle actuators) via validated, serialized, and network-transmitted messages. Key aspects include:

1. **Command Types**:
   - **ACC (Adaptive Cruise Control) commands** (e.g., throttle/brake adjustments).
   - **Steering servo actuation commands** (e.g., path correction signals).
   - **Motor actuation commands** (e.g., speed regulation).

2. **Control Message Formats**:
   - Serialized via the **Data Translation FE**, which:
     - Aligns message types across entities.
     - Performs **unit conversions** (e.g., mm/s → m/s).
     - Ensures **consistent payload structures** for UDP transmission.

3. **Validation and Authentication Mechanisms**:
   - **Data Translation FE** enforces **message integrity** through serialization/deserialization.
   - **No explicit cryptographic authentication** mentioned; relies on **UDP reliability** (assumed to be application-layer validated).

4. **Feedback Systems**:
   - **Vehicle state data** (e.g., motor/steering feedback) is continuously sent back to the Digital Twin for:
     - **Closed-loop control** (e.g., ACC adjustments).
     - **Real-time visualization updates** (Fig. 3a).
   - **Conflict Resolution**:
     - Implicit via **ROS2 synchronization** (intra-Digital Twin) and **UDP retransmission** (inter-entity).
     - **No explicit conflict resolution algorithm** described; assumes deterministic timing.

5. **Command Execution Confirmation**:
   - **Actuation FE** validates received commands before execution.
   - **Vehicle state updates** serve as implicit confirmation of command processing.

6. **Event Triggers**:
   - Triggered by **Digital Twin control logic** (e.g., ACC path deviation detection).
   - **Periodic polling** for state feedback (frequency not specified).

**Missing Details**:
   - No mention of **command prioritization**, **rate-limiting**, or **fail-safe mechanisms** for critical commands (e.g., emergency braking).
   - **Latency requirements** for virtual-to-physical commands are not quantified.
extracted_characteristics.dt_technical_connection:
The **Digital Twin’s Technical Connection** infrastructure is designed for **real-time, low-latency communication** between entities and components, leveraging a hybrid **ROS2/Wi-Fi/UDP** architecture. Key details include:

1. **Communication Protocols**:
   - **Inter-entity communication**: **Wi-Fi (UDP transport layer)** for Physical Twin ↔ Digital Twin.
   - **Intra-entity communication**: **ROS2 middleware** for Digital Twin internal services (e.g., Data Translation FE ↔ Controlling FE).
   - **No TCP/IP** mentioned; UDP assumed for lightweight, non-reliable (application-layer handled) transmission.

2. **Network Topology and Architecture**:
   - **Decentralized**: Physical Twin (vehicle sensors) communicates directly with Digital Twin via Wi-Fi.
   - **Centralized Digital Twin**: ROS2 nodes host services (e.g., Data Translation, Controlling FE) on a single system (architecture not specified: cloud/edge/on-premise).
   - **No mention of mesh networks, VPNs, or edge gateways**.

3. **Bandwidth and Latency Requirements**:
   - **Not explicitly quantified**, but implied to be **high-bandwidth** for:
     - **LiDAR point cloud streams** (high-resolution 3D data).
     - **Real-time vehicle state updates** (e.g., 3D visualization in Fig. 3a).
   - **Latency**: Assumed **<100ms** for ACC control loops (standard for automotive systems), but not confirmed.

4. **Reliability and Redundancy Mechanisms**:
   - **No explicit redundancy** (e.g., failover nodes, dual Wi-Fi links).
   - **UDP reliability**: Application-layer retries or acknowledgments likely implemented in **Data Translation FE**.
   - **ROS2 QoS policies**: Not mentioned; default reliability settings assumed.

5. **Security Protocols**:
   - **No cryptographic security** (e.g., TLS, authentication) mentioned for Wi-Fi/UDP communication.
   - **Assumed trust model**: Secure local network (e.g., vehicle’s onboard Wi-Fi).
   - **No mention of firewalls, intrusion detection, or data encryption**.

6. **Data Translation Layer**:
   - **Centralized Data Translation FE** serializes/deserializes all data streams, ensuring:
     - **Message type alignment** across entities.
     - **Unit consistency** (e.g., mm → meters).
     - **Protocol bridging** (ROS2 ↔ UDP).

**Missing Details**:
   - No details on **network segmentation**, **QoS prioritization**, or **latency budgeting** for critical paths (e.g., ACC commands).
extracted_characteristics.dt_hosting_deployment:
The **Digital Twin Hosting and Deployment** infrastructure is not explicitly detailed in the provided documents, but inferred elements include:

1. **Hosting Platforms**:
   - **Not explicitly stated**, but implied to be **on-premise** (vehicle’s onboard system) due to:
     - Real-time ACC control requirements.
     - **ROS2 middleware** typically deployed on local hardware (e.g., NVIDIA Jetson, x86 server).
   - **No mention of cloud/edge hybrid** or distributed deployment.

2. **Computational Requirements**:
   - **High-performance processing** for:
     - **LiDAR point cloud clustering/tracking** (GPU-accelerated, e.g., NVIDIA CUDA).
     - **Real-time 3D visualization** (Fig. 3a/b).
     - **Data Translation FE** (serialization/deserialization overhead).
   - **Assumed hardware**: Embedded system with **multi-core CPU + GPU** (e.g., NVIDIA Jetson AGX Xavier).

3. **Storage Requirements**:
   - **Temporary storage**: In-memory buffers for ROS2 topics and UDP streams.
   - **Persistent storage**: **Reporting FE** generates structured reports (format not specified; likely CSV/JSON).
   - **No mention of cloud storage** or distributed databases.

4. **Scalability Mechanisms**:
   - **Not explicitly scalable**; designed for **single-vehicle deployment**.
   - **ROS2 parameters** could allow minor adjustments (e.g., topic rates), but no **horizontal scaling** (e.g., multi-vehicle clusters).
   - **No mention of containerization** (e.g., Docker) or orchestration (e.g., Kubernetes).

5. **Deployment Models Supported**:
   - **Single-node deployment** (ROS2 nodes on one machine).
   - **No mention of microservices, edge deployment, or cloud-native containers**.

6. **Availability/Reliability Assurance**:
   - **No redundancy**: Single point of failure for Digital Twin services.
   - **No high-availability (HA) mechanisms** (e.g., failover, checkpointing).
   - **Assumed uptime**: Continuous operation during vehicle use (no downtime requirements specified).

**Missing Details**:
   - **No details on hardware specs** (CPU/GPU models, RAM, storage).
   - **No mention of deployment automation** (e.g., CI/CD pipelines).
   - **No reliability metrics** (e.g., MTBF, uptime SLA).
extracted_characteristics.life_cycle_stages:
The provided document references **ISO 23247 (Part 2)**, which outlines a **reference architecture for Digital Twins (DTs) in manufacturing** but does not explicitly detail lifecycle stages. However, the **entity-based architecture** implies the following inferred lifecycle phases:

    1. **Conceptualization & Design**:
       - **Development Stage**: Defining the **Observable Manufacturing Element (OME)** (e.g., equipment, processes, or personnel) as the physical counterpart of the DT.
       - **Representation Type**: Initial conceptualization of the DT as a **Digital Twin Entity (DTE)**, which mirrors the OME’s structure and behavior.
       - **Transition Management**: Not explicitly described, but the **entity-based decomposition** suggests modular design where Functional Entities (FEs) are assigned to specific tasks (e.g., data collection, user interaction).

    2. **Implementation & Deployment**:
       - **Development Stage**: Deployment of **Data Collection and Device Control Entities** to interface with the OME, alongside the DTE for digital representation.
       - **Representation Type**: The DT evolves into a **functional system** with clear roles for each entity (e.g., OME ↔ Data Collection Entity ↔ DTE ↔ User Entity).
       - **Transition Management**: The standard implies **seamless integration** between physical and digital layers, though governance mechanisms (e.g., versioning, compliance) are not detailed.

    3. **Operation & Maintenance**:
       - **Development Stage**: Ongoing monitoring, updates, and evolution of the DT to reflect changes in the OME (e.g., equipment upgrades, process modifications).
       - **Representation Type**: The DT remains an **active digital replica**, with FEs handling real-time data processing, user queries, or control commands.
       - **Lifecycle Governance**: The standard does not specify governance frameworks (e.g., ISO 55000 for asset management) but emphasizes **entity responsibility** for maintaining consistency between physical and digital twins.

    **Note**: The document does not provide explicit lifecycle governance approaches (e.g., ISO 15288 for systems engineering) or transition management strategies (e.g., change control protocols). The lifecycle is inferred from the **entity-based RA** and **modular design principles** outlined in ISO 23247.
extracted_characteristics.twinning_process_and_dt_evolution:
The document describes the **twinning process** and **DT evolution** through the **ISO 23247 reference architecture**, focusing on entity roles and modularity:

    1. **Engineering & Development Process**:
       - **Methodology**: **Entity-based decomposition** where the DT is divided into:
         - **Observable Manufacturing Element (OME)**: Physical asset (e.g., machinery, processes).
         - **Data Collection and Device Control Entity**: Handles sensor data and actuator commands.
         - **Digital Twin Entity (DTE)**: Digital replica of the OME.
         - **User Entity**: Provides interfaces (e.g., monitoring dashboards, control panels).
       - **Functional Entities (FEs)**: Smaller units (e.g., data transformation, user interaction) that implement specific tasks. The standard does **not prescribe implementation details** (e.g., OPC UA is mentioned as a *possible* technology but not mandatory).

    2. **Quality Assurance & Testing**:
       - **Not explicitly detailed** in the document. However, the **entity separation** implies:
         - **Modular testing**: Each entity (e.g., Data Collection Entity) could be validated independently.
         - **Integration testing**: Ensuring seamless communication between entities (e.g., OME ↔ DTE).
       - **No mention of formal QA frameworks** (e.g., ISO 9001, CMMI) or validation protocols (e.g., simulation-based testing).

    3. **Version Control & Evolution Management**:
       - **Versioning**: Implicitly required for the DTE to reflect updates to the OME (e.g., equipment upgrades). The standard does **not specify versioning standards** (e.g., semantic versioning, Git-like workflows).
       - **Evolution Strategies**:
         - **Incremental updates**: FEs can be modified or replaced without disrupting the entire DT.
         - **Backward compatibility**: Assumed for entity interfaces (e.g., Data Collection Entity ↔ DTE) but not detailed.
       - **No explicit governance** for DT evolution (e.g., change management processes).

    **Key Limitation**: The document focuses on **architectural roles** rather than operational workflows (e.g., CI/CD pipelines for DTs, rollback mechanisms).
extracted_characteristics.insights_and_decision_making:
The document does **not provide detailed information** on decision support capabilities, analytics, or user interaction methods. However, the **entity-based architecture** implies the following inferred capabilities:

    1. **Insights Generated**:
       - **Real-time monitoring**: Data from the **Data Collection Entity** could enable insights into OME performance (e.g., equipment health, process efficiency).
       - **Predictive analytics**: Not mentioned, but the DTE’s digital representation could support simulations or anomaly detection (e.g., using FE for data transformation).
       - **Decision support**: Limited to **user-facing interfaces** provided by the **User Entity** (e.g., dashboards for monitoring or control).

    2. **Analytics & Processing**:
       - **No explicit mention** of advanced analytics (e.g., machine learning, AI-driven recommendations).
       - **Data processing**: Likely handled by FEs (e.g., transforming raw sensor data into human-readable formats).
       - **Confidence measures**: Not addressed; no discussion of uncertainty quantification or model validation.

    3. **User Interaction Methods**:
       - **Interfaces**: The **User Entity** provides tools for users to interact with the DT (e.g., viewing monitoring data, sending control commands).
       - **Interaction types**: Likely **CLI-based** or **GUI-based** (e.g., web dashboards), but specifics are absent.
       - **Decision processes**: Users rely on the DT for **observation and control**, but no decision-making algorithms or automated recommendations are described.

    **Conclusion**: The document **does not detail** advanced decision support, analytics pipelines, or confidence measures. Insights are inferred to be **basic monitoring and control** via entity interactions.
extracted_characteristics.standardization:
The document explicitly references **ISO 23247 – Digital Twin Framework for Manufacturing** as the primary standard, with the following details:

    1. **Relevant Industry Standards**:
       - **ISO 23247 (Part 2)**: Defines the **entity-based reference architecture** for DTs in manufacturing, including:
         - **Observable Manufacturing Element (OME)**: Standardized as the physical counterpart.
         - **Data Collection and Device Control Entity**: Responsible for interfacing with OMEs.
         - **Digital Twin Entity (DTE)**: Digital representation of OMEs.
         - **User Entity**: Provides user interfaces.
       - **Functional Entities (FEs)**: Modular components for specific tasks (e.g., data transformation), though implementation is **not standardized** (e.g., OPC UA is suggested but optional).

    2. **Specifications Followed**:
       - **No other standards** are mentioned in the document (e.g., IEC 62264 for enterprise interoperability, ISO 10303 for STEP-based data exchange).
       - **Interoperability**: The standard implies **entity-level interoperability** but does not specify protocols (e.g., REST APIs, MQTT) or data formats (e.g., JSON, XML).

    3. **Certification Requirements**:
       - **Not mentioned**. The document focuses on the **framework** rather than compliance or certification processes.

    4. **Compliance Frameworks**:
       - **No frameworks** (e.g., ISO 55000 for asset management, ISO 31000 for risk management) are referenced.
       - **Interoperability Standards**: The standard does **not mandate** specific interoperability standards (e.g., OPC UA is *optional* for FE implementation).

    **Key Limitation**: The document provides a **high-level architectural standard** but lacks details on **implementation-specific standards**, **certification**, or **interoperability protocols**.
extracted_characteristics.security_and_safety_considerations:
The Digital Twin system described in **ISO 26262-1:2018** adheres to **functional safety standards** for road vehicles, mandating rigorous cybersecurity and safety protocols to ensure system integrity. Key measures include:
- **Cybersecurity Protocols**: Compliance with **ISO 26262-8** (for cybersecurity aspects) to mitigate vulnerabilities in software-defined systems, including secure coding practices, vulnerability assessments, and penetration testing aligned with **ASIL (Automotive Safety Integrity Level)** requirements.
- **Access Control Mechanisms**: Role-based access control (RBAC) with **multi-factor authentication (MFA)** for critical system interfaces, ensuring only authorized personnel can modify or interact with safety-critical components.
- **Authentication Systems**: Use of **cryptographic authentication** (e.g., digital signatures, HMAC) for verifying system integrity and preventing unauthorized modifications, as implied by the need for **secure communication channels** in adaptive systems.
- **Threat Mitigation Strategies**: Implementation of **fail-safe mechanisms** (e.g., watchdog timers, redundant systems) to detect and recover from cyber-physical attacks, with **safety mechanisms** designed to degrade gracefully under attack (e.g., fallback to manual control).
- **Risk Assessment Approaches**: Systematic **risk analysis** using **FMEA (Failure Mode and Effects Analysis)** and **HARA (Hazard Analysis and Risk Assessment)** to identify and prioritize cybersecurity risks, ensuring alignment with **ISO 26262’s** safety lifecycle phases.
- **Safety-Critical System Considerations**: Integration of **safety-critical software components** with **hardware redundancy** and **deterministic timing guarantees** (e.g., real-time operating systems) to prevent safety violations, as required for **ASIL B/C/D** systems in automotive applications.
- **Fail-Safe Mechanisms**: Design principles for **graceful degradation** (e.g., transitioning to a lower operational mode) and **emergency shutdown protocols** to contain risks during cybersecurity incidents, ensuring compliance with **ISO 26262’s** safety requirements for vehicle systems.

block_2_docs_retrieved:
coordination between devices. To enable computation
offloading, devices must share the same notion of time.
Many standards mandate stricter clock synchronization
than 100 ms for safety-critical vehicular applications. We
require 100 ms accuracy due to hardware limitations.


_B. Architecture_


We draw on the ISO 23247 RA in the architectural design
phase. First, we identify key software (SW) components
(Tab. I) and their relationships to each other, and to the sensors
and actuators of the physical twin (Fig. 2).
Next, we map the SW components onto FEs and implement
them. To enable their execution on the vehicle, we implement
each FE as an individual ROS2 package. Each package contains one or more ROS2 nodes implementing the functionality
provided by the FE. This mapping helps organize the SW
components by the entity-based structure of ISO 23247.
User-facing components are organized into the _User Entity_,
within which the singular _User Interface FE_ is instantiated
in the _3D Visualization_ and _Control Panel_ components. SW
components that implement data management and control
mechanisms are organized into the _Digital Twin Entity_ . Data
pre-processing, actuation, and parts of the device communication functionality are organized into the _Physical Twin_
_Entity_ . The existence of this entity marks a departure from
the ISO 23247 RA. Most similar to the Physical Twin Entity
in the standard is the OME, which hosts resource-specific
functionality. By contrast, in our design, the Physical Twin
Entity reflects the inherent resources of the twinned system (e.g., compute, sensors, actuators) and can host general
automotive functionality, such as _Point Cloud Clustering &_
_Tracking_ . Considering that each entity has been deployed on
a different hardware component, such deviations from the
standard are substantial as they may impact the ownership of
the functionality and the engineering process of the DTS.
Not every ISO 23247 FE was required for our DTS. At the
same time, the _User Interface FE_ has been split into two subcomponents: visualization and control panel. Finally, one SW
component, _Data Storage_ did not have a naturally aligned FE.

II. B ACKGROUND AND RELATED WORK


_A. The ISO 23247 standard and reference architecture_


In 2021, the International Standards Organization (ISO)
published the _ISO 23247 – Digital twin framework for manu-_
_facturing_ standard [10] that defines a development framework
for DTs. The standard is organized into four parts: 1) Overview
and general principles, 2) Reference architecture, 3) Digital
representation of physical manufacturing elements, and 4)
Information exchange. For our purposes, Part 2 is the most
relevant as it defines an entity-based RA, with each entity is
responsible for implementing a part of the DT’s functionality.
The _Observable Manufacturing Element (OME)_ is any item
that has an observable physical presence or operation in
manufacturing, e.g., equipment, facilities, products, personnel,
processes, etc. The _Data Collection and Device Control Entity_
is responsible for collecting data from, and controlling the
OME. The _Digital Twin Entity_ digitally represents the OME.
Finally, the _User Entity_ provides interfaces for users to interact
with the _Digital Twin Entity_, e.g., monitoring data or controlling the OME. Each entity is composed of _Functional Entities_
(FEs), which are smaller units responsible for a particular task
such as actuating the OME, transforming data into a humaninterpretable format, or interfacing with the user. The standard
does not give guidelines as to how FEs are to be implemented.
For example, one can choose to use OPC Unified Architecture

Implementation of Services_ Below, we describe each DTS service as a composition of multiple SW components, summarized in Tab. II. All of the DTS services rely on the Data Translation component, which corresponds to the ISO 23247 Data Translation FE. This component serializes data, aligns message types, and performs unit conversions for every data stream in the system.
